title: OpenAI lifts 2030 compute-spend forecast to $750B, breaks ground on $20B Georgia data center
subtitle: The upward revision from $600B, paired with Project Camellia in Savannah, marks OpenAI's shift from renting compute to building it.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI has raised its projected compute spending through 2030 to about $750 billion, up from the roughly $600 billion figure it had used earlier this year, according to a Wall Street Journal report published July 23. The revision reflects both new infrastructure deals and expanded commitments on existing ones with Microsoft, Oracle, Amazon Web Services and CoreWeave. In parallel, OpenAI is putting $20 billion into a new data center in Savannah, Georgia — code-named Project Camellia — its first meaningful move from renting compute to owning it.

The $750 billion figure is the cash side of what Sam Altman has variously described as $1.15 trillion in hardware and cloud agreements through 2035 and $1.4 trillion in total infrastructure commitments. The gap between those numbers is not sloppy accounting — it is the difference between contracted spend, capital commitments, and stated ambition. What matters is that the contracted floor keeps rising, and every rise puts more pressure on OpenAI to grow revenue fast enough to service the obligations without more equity dilution.

Project Camellia is a strategic shift. Until now OpenAI has run on other people's data centers — Microsoft primarily, then Oracle and the others. Owning racks in Savannah means owning depreciation, power contracts, cooling engineering and substation permits. That is the operating model of a hyperscaler, not an application company, and it puts OpenAI on the same cost curve as its cloud partners. Alphabet's $205 billion capex hike disclosed the same day underscores what that curve costs.

For learners: the story here is not the size of the number, it is what a $750 billion commitment implies about the assumed shape of demand. If OpenAI is spending like this, it expects paid API and product usage to keep compounding fast enough to service the debt and operating costs. Watch the ratio of committed compute to reported revenue over the next four quarters — that ratio is the single clearest signal of whether the frontier AI business model is working. When it starts to compress, either revenue is exceeding plan or spending is being pulled back. Either would be a leading indicator worth catching early.

sources.title: OpenAI lifts planned compute spending to $750 billion through 2030 — WSJ via Yahoo Finance
OpenAI raises cloud spending projection to $750B through 2030 — Crypto Briefing
OpenAI compute spending to reportedly rise from US$600B to US$750B — Digitimes
